evidence: NetMoE proves dynamic sample placement reduces inter-node traffic — RouteWeaver extends this to closed-loop joint optimization with placement + router.
Occult shows expert co-activation can be exploited for collocation — RouteWeaver was to consume the same signal in its medium path.
MoNTA shows network-traffic-aware parallel optimization works offline — RouteWeaver was to extend this to online with congestion feedback.
G2 is the loose-coupling gap; RouteWeaver was the proposed unified runtime.
C1 is RouteWeaver's first precondition: traffic concentration over (src_rank, dst_expert) pairs.
C2 is RouteWeaver's second precondition: clustered co-activation structure.
Top-20% pairs carry only 22.7% of cross-rank tokens (threshold 50%). Near-uniform CDF.
Best silhouette score 0.169 across all layers and k (threshold 0.20). No cluster structure.
Per-domain top-20% shares: wikitext 22.8%, c4 21.9%, mmlu 22.2% — domain-invariant uniformity.
Qwen1.5-MoE top-20%=21.5% — fail also holds for older auxiliary-loss balancing design, not just DeepSeek's loss-free balancing.
Qwen best silhouette=0.186 — slightly higher than DeepSeek but still below 0.20 threshold.
Cross-model comparison shows the failure is universal across two distinct balancing designs (auxiliary-loss vs loss-free) — not a model-specific artifact.
DeepSeek-V3's auxiliary-loss-free balancing is what produces the uniform routing observed in M1.
Pivot direction: probe early-training checkpoints where routing balance has not yet converged.
G4 is the methodology gap exposed by M1+M4 — only converged checkpoints were probed.
OptiReduce demonstrates bounded-loss is publishable for AllReduce; lossbound_ep extends to AllToAll with token-level value.
MegaBlocks proves token dropping hurts quality, motivating value-aware (rather than uniform) drop policies.
G1 is the token-semantics-as-citizen gap; lossbound_ep makes per-token value drive transport decisions.
MegaScale-Infer's M2N library shows expert-parallel collectives need a custom primitive, not just NCCL.
G3 is the portable EP primitive gap; hybridep_collective defines a unified API across exact/approx modes.
Pre-gated MoE exploits cross-layer expert predictability as a method but never measures it as a phenomenon — opens the measurement question.
G6 is exactly the joint-distribution-vs-marginal-balance gap in load-balancing objectives.
C3 is the empirical precondition: cross-layer top-1 expert MI exceeds shuffled-null by ≥0.3 nat.
Qwen MI−null=1.534 nat (39.5% of H(L+1)); DeepSeek MI−null=0.686 nat (22.0%). Both exceed 0.3-nat strong threshold by 2-5×.
Hypothesis confirmed across two distinct models with different balancing schools and different top-k.
Reuses M1 trace data — zero additional GPU cost. The same trace that killed routeweaver feeds the new live idea.
Reuses M4 trace data — zero additional GPU cost.
OLMoE MI-strict-null=1.212 nat (30.5% of H(L+1)); 92.5% of sources reduce entropy ≥0.5 nat. Triangulates Qwen vs DeepSeek to isolate balancing scheme as dominant factor.
Confirms phenomenon on a third model family (allenai OLMoE) with different top-k and depth. Universality of phenomenon strengthened.
Adds the third design-cube corner (top-8 aux-loss) to round-1's two-model comparison.
OLMoE chosen as the triangulation point because it has different top-k (8) and aux-loss balancing (vs DeepSeek's loss-free) — clean confound isolator.
OLMoE's aux-loss balancing → high cross-layer MI, confirming balancing scheme as dominant predictor.
G7 (why aux-loss-free reduces cross-layer MI) was OPENED by this triangulation.
DeepSeek-V3's loss-free balancing is the source of the lower cross-layer MI signal observed in exp:m1+m6.
C4 is the multi-domain extension of C3: tests whether the cross-layer MI phenomenon is domain-invariant or domain-dependent.
9-of-9 (model, domain) cells exceed 0.3-nat strong threshold; 3-of-3 models show code as lowest-MI domain. Phenomenon survives but is domain-dependent.
Phenomenon robust across code, math, NL on three architectures. Domain modulates magnitude but not direction.
Reuses m1/m4/m7 NL traces; adds 6 new (model, domain) cells.
Reuses OLMoE NL trace; adds OLMoE × {code, math}.
Opens G8 — why is code universally the lowest-MI domain across 3 architectures?
Strengthens G7: DeepSeek has BOTH lowest absolute MI (round 2) AND smallest domain spread (round 3). Single flattening mechanism likely.

Disambiguation: code router entropy is +0.06 to +0.20 nat above NL (G8a partially supported). Filtered-MI test shows OOD explains 15-60% of cross-domain MI gap depending on model; residual is structural (G8b).
Joins entropy data with M8 routing traces for filtered-MI test using same data + seed alignment.
Confirms code is genuinely structurally different on OLMoE (0.49 nat residual gap after controlling for confidence) — claim:C4's domain dependence is real, not just measurement noise.
Wraps Pre-gated MoE-style cross-layer predictor with a confidence gate. Predictor trusts only when entropy < threshold.
exp:m9 quantifies the dividend: confidence gate recovers 15-60% of cross-domain MI loss, depending on model.
Targets the OOD component of G8. The structural residual (esp. OLMoE) requires a separate domain-conditional predictor.

Opens G9 — OLMoE has lowest published code share (~2.5%, OLMoE paper Table 2) yet largest structural residual (0.49 nat); confidence-gating barely shrinks the gap (15%). Falsifies in-distribution-mix hypothesis; supports saturated-router hypothesis (OLMoE NL median entropy 3.812 nat is highest of the three).
Pivoted from the H1 measurement finding (22-40% MI in 9/9 cells) to a system-paper grafting attempt on COMET/MegaScale-Infer.
Intended integration target — pipelined MoE runtime with random/round-robin placement default.
Targets the same per-layer load-balancing gap as the parent idea — converting cross-layer MI into spatial placement gain.

ExFlow (Jan 2024) already solves cross-layer-aware placement via integer programming on conditional cross-layer routing probabilities; reports up to 67% cross-GPU routing latency reduction. The novelty claim of the pivot collapses.

MoETuner (Feb 2025) uses two-stage ILP for cross-layer placement, reports 9.3% single-node / 17.5% multi-node end-to-end speedup on Mixtral-8x7B. Mathematically dominates our greedy trajectory simulator on the shared pipelined-cost objective.
Aug 2025 follow-up to MoETuner that adds datacenter topology cost — pre-empts what was the Plan C extension in our killed brainstorm.
MoETuner builds on ExFlow's cross-layer affinity insight with a two-stage ILP that adds explicit load balancing.
Replaces MoETuner's load-equality objective with topology-weighted datacenter traffic minimization subject to balance constraints.
Exploits cross-layer joint structure P(e_L, e_{L+1}) directly as a placement signal.
Two-stage ILP uses inter-layer affinity matrix as input.
Inherits MoETuner's cross-layer signal usage.
